title: Install the latest version
body:
### Feature request description

I use `Rocky Linux 10` and the latest available version of `Podman` is `5.4.0` in `dnf`.
It's good to have a script like `Rootless Docker` to install the latest version of `Podman` without package managers.

```sh
# https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/rootless/
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com/rootless | sh
```

### Suggest potential solution

None

### Have you considered any alternatives?

None

### Additional context

None

comments.body: this is an interesting request.  the problem is that unlike docker, podman has many binary dependencies like crun or conmon.  we can also alternatively use different runtimes like libkrun.  this means this is not a simple script now.  wdyt ?

I think we had basically the same conversations many times around ubuntu/debian builds, refer to my comment here https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/25219#discussioncomment-13168816 

Distro builds get properly tested and integrated with the rest of the system while this one would be not.

I don't think we are in a position where we want to take on such work to maintain static binaries that work on all distros, first as @baude said we depend on many different dependencies but also we depend on many dynamically linked libraries on the system so doing this right isn't so simple.

And lastly personally `curl | sh` is a anti pattern IMO that I would not want encourage anyone to use to install podman.

For static binaries there is actually someone maintaining them here https://github.com/mgoltzsche/podman-static (not a recommendation for or against using it)
For actual packages for Fedora and RHEL/Centos and its derivatives there is the podman-next copr which comes with the latest build from main so that can be used to validate all the recent code not just releases https://copr.fedorainfracloud.org/coprs/rhcontainerbot/podman-next/

So IMO I would close this as "won't do" unless other maintainers disagree for some reason.

> unlike docker, podman has many binary dependencies like crun or conmon.

I think the dependencies are very similar, when compared with runc and containerd?

----

But I also think that static linking is harder, so I would at least use dynamic linking...
For my own build, I used system libraries because it was easier and ended up smaller.

```
libseccomp2
libglib2.0-0
libsystemd0
libcap2
libyajl2
libgpgme11
```

I did not build krun. The result is very similar in size to docker, 69M compared to 79M.
The previous packages used Nix, so they were bigger (especially when building, 10G!)

podman: v5.6.1
conmon: v2.1.13
crun: 1.24
netavark: v1.16.1
passt: 2025_09_11.6cbcccc
catatonit: v0.2.1
aardvark-dns: v1.16.0

* #23743
* #25219

```
$ ldd /usr/bin/podman
	linux-vdso.so.1 (0x00007f483b174000)
	libresolv.so.2 => /lib64/libresolv.so.2 (0x00007f483b140000)
	libsubid.so.4 => /lib64/libsubid.so.4 (0x00007f483b136000)
	libsqlite3.so.0 => /lib64/libsqlite3.so.0 (0x00007f483afc0000)
	libgpgme.so.11 => /lib64/libgpgme.so.11 (0x00007f48385ac000)
	libseccomp.so.2 => /lib64/libseccomp.so.2 (0x00007f483af9d000)
	libgcc_s.so.1 => /lib64/libgcc_s.so.1 (0x00007f483857d000)
	libc.so.6 => /lib64/libc.so.6 (0x00007f483838a000)
	libaudit.so.1 => /lib64/libaudit.so.1 (0x00007f4838352000)
	libselinux.so.1 => /lib64/libselinux.so.1 (0x00007f4838323000)
	libsemanage.so.2 => /lib64/libsemanage.so.2 (0x00007f48382e3000)
	libeconf.so.0 => /lib64/libeconf.so.0 (0x00007f483af8d000)
	libcrypt.so.2 => /lib64/libcrypt.so.2 (0x00007f48382ad000)
	libacl.so.1 => /lib64/libacl.so.1 (0x00007f483af83000)
	libattr.so.1 => /lib64/libattr.so.1 (0x00007f48382a5000)
	libpam.so.0 => /lib64/libpam.so.0 (0x00007f4838293000)
	libpam_misc.so.0 => /lib64/libpam_misc.so.0 (0x00007f483828d000)
	libm.so.6 => /lib64/libm.so.6 (0x00007f48381a5000)
	libz.so.1 => /lib64/libz.so.1 (0x00007f4838181000)
	libassuan.so.0 => /lib64/libassuan.so.0 (0x00007f483816c000)
	libgpg-error.so.0 => /lib64/libgpg-error.so.0 (0x00007f4838143000)
	/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 (0x00007f483b176000)
	libcap-ng.so.0 => /lib64/libcap-ng.so.0 (0x00007f483813b000)
	libpcre2-8.so.0 => /lib64/libpcre2-8.so.0 (0x00007f4838099000)
	libsepol.so.2 => /lib64/libsepol.so.2 (0x00007f4837fd6000)
	libbz2.so.1 => /lib64/libbz2.so.1 (0x00007f4837fc2000)
```

That is on a fedora rpm build, yes you can omit some of them but then you also loose features, i.e. libsubid, so this is not something I would like to distribute as static builds. And as dynamically linked ones they could only work on one distro more or less.

In general I don't think upstream maintainers should be responsible for providing builds for all kind of environments, it is just to much work without meaningful gain to an upstream dev. After all all our tools are FOSS so anyone can build and distribute them really to their liking.

> I use `Rocky Linux 10` and the latest available version of `Podman` is `5.4.0` in `dnf`.

I think this was regarded as a feature, if you want the latest there are CentOS and Fedora.

---

Distro | Package
--- | ---
Fedora 42 | `podman-5.6.2-1.fc42`
CentOS Stream 10 | `podman-5.6.0-2.el10`
AlmaLinux 10 | `podman-5.4.0-13.el10_0`

https://repology.org/project/podman/badges

> It's good to have a script like `Rootless Docker` to install the latest version of `Podman` without package managers.
> 
>     # https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/rootless/
>     curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com/rootless | sh

There's one thing I'll never understand of this:
* you're downloading a third-party script and running it without reading it, instead of relying on a distribution with all the related scrutiny and security effort behind it
* you do that so that you can run untrusted containers without root
* ??
* no, no profit...

I concur with @Luap99, I don't think this is something this repo should support.

Having maintained some older systems in the past I understand the frustration of using older versions of software with bugs that have been fixed upstream etc.

IMO the best solution would be using the COPR build of latest, you will also be helping the project by potentially catching any bugs introduced into main.

Podman is open source you could create and maintain a script yourself and you may find users less security conscious than us that would love to use it.
it seems that this is a feature we will implement upstream.  if you feel we should reconsider or there is a change of heart by maintainers, we can certainly re-open.

> it seems that this is a feature we will implement upstream.

You mean *will not*...?
Is there any reason why there is no copr repo that instead of building on top of the main branch uses the latest release?
